data.title: Brazilian minister intervenes in JBS slave labor investigation
data.description:
Key points:
  • Brazilian Labor Minister to review JBS case in unprecedented decision
  • JBS Aves accused of keeping workers in 'slavery-like conditions'
  • Intervention raises concerns about political influence in anti-slavery efforts

By Fabio Teixeira

Brazilian Labor Minister Luiz Marinho is carrying out an unusual final review of an investigation that could blacklist a poultry unit of meatpacker JBS SA (JBSS3.SA) for subjecting workers to "slavery-like conditions," according to documents seen by Reuters.

That interruption of the usual process has stoked concerns among labor inspectors and legal experts who called it an unprecedented move that could introduce political influence into Brazil's decades-long effort to combat modern slavery.

The Labor Ministry did not respond to a detailed set of questions, but said the proceeding is ongoing and appeals by JBS are still under analysis.

The case stems from a federal raid last year that found ten people working in slavery-like conditions for a contractor hired to load and unload cargo for a JBS poultry unit called JBS Aves, in the state of Rio Grande do Sul.

Inspectors found that workers were subjected to illegally long shifts for as long as 16 hours and housed without access to clean drinking water, according to a report seen by Reuters. The contractor had also made unlawful deductions from workers' wages, making it harder for them to quit, the report found.

In a statement, JBS told Reuters on Thursday it immediately suspended the contractor, terminated the contract and blocked the company upon learning of the allegations. "The company has zero tolerance for labor and human rights violations," the statement added.

Labor inspectors ruled on August 6 that JBS was responsible for the working conditions of the ten employees, as it failed to conduct due diligence ensuring the contractor was treating them lawfully.

Typically, such a ruling would result in the inclusion of the company in a list of employers responsible for submitting workers to slavery-like conditions, known as "the dirty list," which is scheduled to be updated in October.

Once a company is included in the list, it stays there for two years. Beyond the reputational risks associated with the listing, companies are also barred from obtaining certain types of loans from Brazilian banks, which could mean serious financial consequences for a company linked to one of Brazil's largest firms.

WIDE REPERCUSSIONS

After the inspectors' ruling in August, a legal opinion by Brazil's solicitor general's office (AGU), seen by Reuters, found that the minister could pull the proceedings for his own review, citing the stature of JBS in the Brazilian economy.

JBS is one of Brazil's largest employers, with some 158,000 workers in the country, according to the company. Its Seara division, which runs JBS Aves, reported net revenue of $2.2 billion from April to June, about a tenth of the firm's total.

Adding JBS Aves to the slave labor list would have "repercussions of wide magnitude, with direct repercussions on the company's assets, its commercial relations, its image in the market and, ultimately, could generate a significant impact on the economic sector itself at the national level," AGU wrote.

AGU did not immediately reply to a request for comment.

On Monday, Marinho pulled the proceeding for his review, according to a document seen by Reuters.

Such a move is unprecedented in over two decades that a dedicated task force has been managing Brazil's slave labor blacklist, labor inspectors and legal experts told Reuters.

The minister's move has caused "deep bewilderment and concern" among those who work directly on slave labor cases, said Renato Barbedo Futuro, the president of the association of federal labor inspectors in Rio Grande do Sul, in a public statement.

The ministry's decision could have consequences beyond the JBS case, as it sets precedent for other firms to petition the minister to intervene in their cases, said Livia Miraglia, a labor law professor at the Federal University of Minas Gerais who specializes in slave labor cases for over 20 years.

"I have never seen anything like it," she said.

data.title: U.K. Consumer Sentiment Falls Back
data.description:
By Ed Frankl

The mood of British consumers dampened a little this month, with expectations of a slowing economy and the potential for tax increases later this year threatening an even greater downturn.

The headline level of consumer confidence fell back by two points to minus 19 in September, according to research group GfK's monthly index, published with the Nuremberg Institute for Market Decisions on Friday.

The Bank of England's interest-rate cut in August didn't provide any obvious boost to the financial mood of consumers or draw attention away from day-to-day cost issues, said Neil Bellamy, consumer insights director at GfK.

The central bank held rates on Thursday, with investors split on whether it will cut again before the end of the year.

All five components of the consumer confidence index--gauging the situation of personal finances, desire to make major purchases and views of the economy--pointed down this month, the survey said.

"With tax rises expected in the November budget, the risk is that confidence inevitably falls," Bellamy added.

Write to Ed Frankl at edward.frankl@wsj.com

data.title: FedEx Predicts $1 Billion Tariffs Hit to Fiscal 2026 Earnings — WSJ
data.description:
By Esther Fung

Parcel giant FedEx said it expects a $1 billion hit to its earnings this fiscal year because of changes in the global trade environment stemming from U.S. tariffs. In the prior fiscal year ended in May, FedEx reported $6.1 billion in adjusted operating income.

The company said parcel volume from China to the U.S. continues to shrink. FedEx reduced its Trans-Pacific Asia outbound capacity by 25% year-over-year in the quarter ended in August, and by 10% from the prior quarter. The drop in demand is primarily because of a policy change by the Trump administration ending the de minimis provision that previously exempted packages valued at $800 or less from tariffs.

"It's been really stressful for customers," said Brie Carere, FedEx's chief customer officer, adding that smaller businesses have found navigating the end of de minimis more challenging and that FedEx is helping them automate some customs-clearance processes.

For the coming peak season, Carere said that FedEx is "cautiously optimistic" about parcel volumes, adding that U.S. consumers still appear resilient.

FedEx said it expects revenue to grow 4% to 6% in the fiscal year 2026, and operating income to be largely flat.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1911 ET [Dow Jones]--Morgan Stanley estimates ANZ needs to find up to A$2.0 billion of cost savings if it wants to achieve likely medium-term targets under new CEO Nuno Matos. Analyst Richard E. Wiles thinks twin goals of 11-12% return on equity and a CTI ratio of less than 48% within three years would be plausible. MS says its forecasts already assume cost savings totaling A$1.15 billion, including from changes to simplify the bank. "We estimate that even greater savings of A$1.5 billion-A$2.0 billion would require cost reduction of 15-20% in Australian banking and the group center, with a reduction of 10.0-12.5% in the Institutional and New Zealand divisions," MS says. Its price target rises 2.7% to A$30.50/share. ANZ ended Thursday at A$32.86. (david.winning@wsj.com)

1858 ET [Dow Jones]--Santos's share price is likely to remain volatile in the near term as investors adjust to the Australian energy company no longer being a bid target of the XRG consortium, Morgan Stanley says. Investors in Santos must digest several uncertainties, including around domestic energy supply and the decommissioning of some assets. It will take time for fundamentals, such as Santos's free cash flow yield, to reassert themselves, analyst Rob Koh says. MS cuts its price target on Santos by 21% to A$7.00/share, but retains an equal-weight call. Karoon Energy presents the most upside for investors among Australian energy stocks, MS says. Santos ended Thursday at A$6.74, down 12% across that session after the XRG consortium withdraw its bid proposal. (david.winning@wsj.com)

1830 ET [Dow Jones]--Australia's S&P/ASX looks set to stay on course for a third consecutive weekly loss despite being poised to pare its recent losses when the local share market opens. ASX futures are up by 0.5% ahead of Friday's session, suggesting the benchmark index will follow U.S. equities higher. However, the ASX 200 is down by almost 1.4% so far this week and is likely to notch its longest run of weekly losses since March. Ahead of the open, Perseus said it secured approvals for development and mining at its Yaoure Gold Mine. Tech stocks pushed all three major U.S. indexes to records. The Nasdaq Composite Index gained 0.9%, the S&P 500 added 0.5%, and the DJIA rose 0.3%. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1609 ET - U.S. stocks rise to new highs after the Fed cuts rates and signals more to come this year. Nvidia's deal to invest $5 billion in beleaguered chipmaker Intel boosts tech shares. Intel gains 23% and Nvidia rises 3.5%. Consumer staples shares fall 1%. The Trump administration asks the Supreme Court to let it remove Fed governor Lisa Cook from the central bank's board while a lawsuit challenging the president's effort to fire her proceeds. DJIA rises 124 points, or 0.3%, to 46142, the S&P 500 climbs 0.5% to 6631 and the Nasdaq gains 0.9% to 22470. (patrick.sullivan@wsj.com)

1422 ET - Workday's core business continues to pose a large growth opportunity, D.A. Davidson analysts Gil Luria and Lucky Schreiner say in a note. The company's management sees a large addressable market of $188 billion for its core products, compared to $160 billion last year, given under penetrated markets with U.S. federal customers and frontline workers, the analysts say. The company is also working to simplify its platform to gain traction with mid-sized enterprises, which represent over half of the total available market, they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1418 ET - Workday's new product announcements and financial disclosures put it in a better position to reap the benefits of growing agentic AI adoption across human resources, D.A. Davidson analysts Gil Luria and Lucky Schreiner say in a note. Workday is making it easier for customers to adopt new AI agents, and has been able to buy AI tools that can work off its curated, high quality enterprise data, the analysts say. It also requires agents to have a measured return on investment before release, they say. However, with some of its new AI agents set for release next year, further revenue growth will come mostly from acquisitions in the meantime, limiting upside to near-term numbers, they say. They raise their price target on stock to $260 from $225.Workday is down 0.6% to $233.57. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1408 ET - Sangoma Technologies' return to growth is taking longer than expected, TD Cowen's David Kwan says in a report as the stock sinks 14% to C$7.27 after FY26 guidance came in soft. Sangoma's revenue outlook of C$200 million-C$210 million was 4% below expectations at midpoint, the analyst says. However, the stock may already have poor performance baked in. "We believe this is mostly reflected in the current discount valuation," the analyst says. Shares are down 27% year-to-date, which Kwan says makes the valuation "attractive, along with its [roughly] 15% free cash flow yield," as "execution and visibility are improving." (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1319 ET - Widespread use of GLP-1 drugs may lead to more energy drink sales, say Stifel analysts, citing a survey of consumers. Respondents of the survey that use a GLP-1 drug were 42% more likely to consume an energy drink at least once per month compared with the general population. "We think this reflects the need for greater caffeine as the loss of appetite caused by the GLP-1 reduces consumption of calories that are converted to energy," the analysts say. Increased availability of GLP-1 drugs, particularly among people aged 18 to 34--the age range with the most energy drink consumption--could benefit energy drink sales, the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1308 ET - CrowdStrike's new solutions and features show it continues to innovate at an impressive pace, Junaid Siddiqui of Truist Securities says in a note. The company introduced seven new AI agents to automate security workflows at its recent conference, as well as a no-code platform for customers to build their own security agents, among other innovations, the analyst says. Those new solutions and features should help the company to benefit from a number of continued tailwinds, including vendor consolidation and a heightened threat environment, Siddiqui says, raising the price target to $550 from $500. CrowdStrike climbs 11% at $496.34.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1250 ET - CrowdStrike's flexible subscription licensing model for its Falcon platform appears to be boosting the company's traction with both enterprise and small- and mid-sized businesses, JPMorgan analysts say in a note. Flex remained a central theme throughout the company's recent conference, both in conversations with customers and partners as well as during presentations, the analysts say. "Investor sessions and customer panels reinforced CrowdStrike's position as the 'oxygen' of cybersecurity, with Flex contracts driving deeper module adoption, larger deal sizes, and streamlined procurement," they say. CrowdStrike surges 11%.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1234 ET - Consumers' expectations for their future discretionary spending are improving, even for low-income consumers, say Stifel analysts, citing a survey of consumers. The mid-September survey shows net discretionary spending intentions, calculated as the difference between the percentage of responsentd planning to spend more and the percentage planning to spend less, are up 10% from August, 20% from July and 36% year-over-year. Spending intentions among those earning less than $50,000 improved by 12% from August to September. "Our survey shows U.S. consumer spending intentions continue to improve and exceed historical averages," the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1200 ET — CrowdStrike remains the "gold standard" in cybersecurity, with its new AI products positioning it to lead the cybersecurity industry in agentic AI, Wedbush Securities analysts say in a note. That's especially important as organizations face increasingly complex cybersecurity threats, with a majority of organizations expecting a 20% increase in non-human interactions next year, the analysts say. "With the threat landscape now allowing conventionally underskilled adversaries to leverage AI and increase sophistication of malware, CRWD provides security teams necessary tools to combat these attacks by positioning its customers for the modern battlefield," they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

data.title: New Zealand Stocks Rise But Point to Weekly Decline
data.description:
The NXZ 50 rose by 18 points, or 0.1%, to 13,138 in Friday morning trade, moving up from its lowest level in two weeks, touched the day before.

The rise tracked an upbeat session on Wall Street overnight, supported by the Fed rate cuts and indicating two additional cuts before the end of the year.

Optimism increased following a statement from New Zealand's Prime Minister Christopher Luxon, who reiterated on Friday that he has huge confidence in Nicola Willis as finance minister and in economic growth.

On the data front, the country’s trade gap narrowed in August compared to the same month last year, despite being larger than expected.

Communication services, utilities, and industrials mainly drove the index, with notable gains from F&C Investment (0.9%), Ebos Group (0.8%), ANZ Group (0.5%), and Delegat Group (0.5%).

For the week, the index is heading for a 0.7% fall after being flat the previous week amid disappointing GDP results.

data.title: Treasury yields rise after Fed rate cut, with Powell in no 'sprint' to loosen policy
data.description:
By Christine Idzelis

The housing market is 'stuck' and the labor market is 'losing velocity,' says BlackRock's deputy CIO of fixed income

Treasury yields have gone up since the Fed announced Wednesday that it decided to cut its benchmark interest rate.

Treasury bond yields continued rising Thursday, with investors in the bond market adjusting their positioning in the aftermath of the Federal Reserve's shift in monetary policy.

The yield on the 10-year Treasury note BX:TMUBMUSD10Y rose 3.1 basis points to 4.104%, while the 2-year Treasury rate BX:TMUBMUSD02Y edged up 2.1 basis points to 3.567%, according to Dow Jones Market Data. That's after yields ended higher Wednesday, following Fed Chair Jerome Powell's press conference on the central bank's decision to resume its rate-cutting cycle.

Although the Fed's decision to lower its policy rate by a quarter-percentage point was widely expected, there had been some "creeping hope" in the market for a larger "surprise" cut of a half-percentage point, said Russ Brownback, deputy chief investment officer of fixed income at BlackRock, in a phone interview. Also, the Fed's messaging "could have signaled a bit more of a dovish-forward view," considering the labor market has weakened and tariffs don't appear to be creating "broader knock-on effects on inflation," he said.

Brownback described the Fed's real policy rate as "very restrictive," saying he had wished to see a more dovish stance that leaned more in favor of further rate cuts. "It's becoming increasingly obvious that this policy setting is hurting millions of households," he said. "The housing market is stuck" and the labor market is "losing velocity," Brownback explained.

Ahead of the Fed's rate decision Wednesday, traders in the federal-funds futures market had priced in a small probability that the Fed could cut its policy rate by a half-percentage point, according to the CME FedWatch Tool. The Fed's decision to shave off a quarter-percentage point left its policy rate at a current target range of 4% to 4.25%.

Heading into its policy meeting, the Fed's rate was higher on a "real basis," adjusted for inflation, than when the central bank last hiked in July 2023, according to Brownback. The Fed hiked its policy rate at the time in a "hawkish" effort to bring down inflation when it was more elevated than it is currently. "They're tighter today than they were then" with the respect to the "real policy rate," and parts of the economy are suffering, he said.

Although markets are "telling you that growth is fine" in the U.S. economy generally, it's "suboptimal" as households with lower levels of income and wealth are struggling, according to Brownback. One way that a lower policy rate might help is that it would bring some relief to credit-card borrowers, he said.

Meanwhile, inflation in the U.S. is down significantly from its 2022 peak, but it remains above the Fed's 2% target.

Next week, on Sept 26, investors will get a reading on U.S. inflation from the personal-consumption expenditures price index for the month of August. Core PCE, the Fed's preferred inflation gauge that excludes food and energy prices, rose 2.9% in the 12 months through July.

'Not in a sprint'

Fed officials penciled in two more rate cuts of a quarter-percentage point each by year's end, according to the central bank's Summary of Economic Projections released on Wednesday.

Fed funds futures on Thursday reflected a high probability of that outcome, and indicated that traders were leaning toward the Fed's policy rate potentially falling further to 3%-3.25% by the end of next June, the CME FedWatch Tool showed, at last check.

Ahead of Wednesday's Fed meeting, "markets had priced too much into the path" of potential cuts, with rates in the bond market rising afterwards as a result, according to a note Wednesday from MUFG's head of U.S. macro strategy George Goncalves.

He described the Fed as delivering a "hawkish-leaning cut," saying Powell noted the lack of widespread support at its policy meeting for a more aggressive half-point reduction.

The bond market began adjusting Wednesday to the idea the Fed is "not in a sprint" toward lowering its policy rate to a "neutral" level that neither restricts nor stimulates the economy, according to his note. "Powell went out of his way to not send overtly dovish signals," said Goncalves. "This was as hawkish as he could have been, especially given that they just cut rates and lowered their rates dot path a bit."

Exchange-traded funds that invest in Treasurys saw $186 million of outflows on Wednesday, even as fixed-income ETFs broadly attracted $702 million, according to a CreditSights report. High-yield bond ETFs lost $66 million that same day, the report shows.

Long-term Treasury yields rose faster than short-term rates on Thursday.

As a result, the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF TLT was under more selling pressure than funds focused on the shorter end of the bond market's yield curve, falling a sharp 1%. Bond yields and prices move in opposite directions. Within the belly of the curve, the iShares 3-7 Year Treasury Bond ETF IEI slipped a slight 0.1% on Thursday.

Brownback said that over the course of this year, the front and the "belly" of the yield curve have been more convincing hedges within fixed-income than long-term rates.

Beyond the central bank's shift Wednesday on the short-term fed-funds rate, Brownback said he had been watching the outcome of the Fed's policy meeting for some signal that it may be on a path toward ending quantitative tightening, or the reduction of its balance sheet. That's because of his concern about indications of modest "stresses" in the repo market, but there was no change to the Fed's balance-sheet policy, he said.

-Christine Idzelis

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Markets Headlines at 7 PM ET: Tech Deal, Rate Cut Push Stocks to Records | U.S. ...
data.description:
Tech Deal, Rate Cut Push Stocks to Records

Intel has best day since 1987 after Nvidia invests $5 billion.

----

U.S. Leading Indicators Continue to Show Weakening Economy

The U.S. economy faces a slowing economy amid growing concerns about the labor market, headwinds from tariffs and a weak manufacturing sector, according to a basket of economic indicators.

----

Trump's Trade War Split Canada and Mexico. Now They Want a United Front.

America's top two trading partners are seeking a strategic partnership ahead of negotiations with the U.S. over tariffs.

----

U.S. Jobless Claims Fell Last Week

The number of Americans who newly filed for unemployment benefits declined sharply last week, returning the figures to the previous trend after a large spike the week before.

----

Florida Orange Output Lowest Since the Great Depression

The amount of oranges grown in the state has fallen 32% from the previous year and 82% since the beginning of the decade, according to the USDA.

----

Trump Administration Asks Supreme Court to Allow Removal of Fed Governor Lisa Cook

A lower court blocked Cook's firing while she challenges the lawfulness of her termination.

----

Bank of England Leaves Key Rate on Hold, Slows Quantitative Tightening

The U.K.'s central bank left its key rate at 4% as policymakers are growing increasingly worried about a pickup in inflation.

----

The Fed Rolls Back Recession Risk

The central bank's quarter-point rate cut has eliminated the U.S. economy's worst-case scenarios.

----

Norges Bank Cuts Key Rate But Hints at Very Gradual Easing Going Forward

The central bank cut its rate to 4% and said further easing would likely be needed for economic growth, as policymakers expect one rate cut a year in the coming three years.

----

South African Reserve Bank Maintains Main Repo Rate at 7%

The decision pauses the cutting cycle it began in September when the bank started to reduce rates from a 15-year high of 8.25%.

----

Taiwan Central Bank Leaves Key Interest Rates Unchanged

The Taiwanese central bank held rates steady again, as the island's strong economic performance so far this year gave it room to extend its pause.

----

ECB Doesn't Know If Easing Cycle Is Over, De Guindos Says

The ECB's vice president said that, while the risk that inflation will fall below the 2% target over the medium term wasn't big, a further cut in the key rate couldn't be ruled out.

----

What Decides Where Mortgage Rates Go From Here

While the Federal Reserve has begun cutting rates again, there are many other factors that affect the cost of home loans.

----

Bank of Japan Meets as New Uncertainties Emerge

Hopes for a rate increase rose rapidly after Tokyo reached a trade deal with the U.S. in July, but those expectations faded earlier this month when Prime Minister Shigeru Ishiba announced his resignation.

----

Irish Central Bank Raises Growth Forecasts on U.S. Tariff Agreement

The tariffs on imports of goods set by the U.S. pose less of a threat to the future growth of the Irish economy than feared.

Cracker Barrel Targeted With Proxy Fight By Steak 'n Shake Investor

FTC Sues Ticketmaster Alleging Illegal Ticket Resale Tactics

New Owners Plot a Comeback for Tween Retailer Claire's

Darden Raises Sales Outlook, Warns Rising Costs Will Squeeze Profit

Inside Microsoft's Plans for the 'Most Advanced AI Data Center in the World'

----

DuPont Cuts Full-Year Sales Guidance to Reflect Divestiture, Spinoff

DuPont cut its full-year sales guidance to reflect its previously-announced divestiture of its Aramids business along with the spinoff of its electronics unit.

----

Novo Nordisk Says Wegovy Weight-Loss Pill Matches Shot as it Eyes Landmark Approval

The company's experimental daily pill helped patients lose an average 16.6% of their weight, according to late-stage trial, and it could be approved in the U.S. by the end of the year.

----

Roche to Buy Maker of Fatty Liver Treatment 89bio for Up to $3.5 Billion

The deal seeks to strengthen the drugmaker's pipeline by adding an experimental treatment for a liver disease linked to obesity.

UK consumers turn more wary as economic worries mount, GfK says
data.description:
Britons became more gloomy this month and the prospect of tax increases in finance minister Rachel Reeves' budget in November threatens to damage confidence levels further, a survey showed on Friday.

Market research firm GfK said its consumer confidence index sank to -19 in September from -17 in August which had been its highest since December, and slightly below the -18 forecast in a Reuters poll.

Neil Bellamy, consumer insights director at GfK, said all five measures of confidence dipped this month and a separate gauge of savings intention fell sharply.

An index measuring sentiment about the economic outlook now stands at -32, way down from -11 in June last year, shortly before Prime Minister Keir Starmer's Labour Party won a parliamentary election.

"With tax rises expected in the November budget, the risk is that confidence inevitably falls," Bellamy said.

Reeves is expected to raise taxes in her annual fiscal statement on November 26 in order to remain on course to meet her budget targets, having ordered employers to pay higher social security contributions in October last year.

The GfK survey was based on a poll of 2,003 individuals conducted between September 1 and September 11.
